PT_AREAS.label: Shoulder / Rotator Cuff
PT_AREAS.overview: The rotator cuff is four muscles that stabilize your shoulder joint. Pain usually comes from impingement, weakness in the small stabilizers, or poor scapular control. The goal is to decompress the joint, strengthen the external rotators, and rebuild scapular stability.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid heavy overhead pressing, upright rows, and behind-the-neck movements until pain-free.

stretches.name: Cross-Body Shoulder Stretch
stretches.cue: Pull one arm across chest with opposite hand. Keep shoulder blades level. Feel the rear delt and posterior capsule stretch.

stretches.name: Sleeper Stretch
stretches.cue: Lie on your side on a mat. Bend elbow 90 deg, use opposite hand to gently push forearm toward the floor. This stretches the posterior capsule -- a common tightness with shoulder pain.

stretches.name: Doorway Pec Stretch
stretches.cue: Forearms on a doorframe at 90 deg. Lean forward until you feel a deep chest stretch. Tight pecs pull the shoulder forward and contribute to impingement.

stretches.name: Overhead Towel Stretch
stretches.cue: Hold a towel behind your back -- one hand above, one below. Gently pull the lower hand down to stretch the upper shoulder's internal rotation.

stretches.name: Child's Pose with Rotation
stretches.cue: In child's pose, thread one arm underneath your body. This decompresses the shoulder and stretches the posterior capsule simultaneously.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation
exercises.cue: Anchor a band at elbow height. Elbow bent 90 deg, tucked to side. Rotate forearm outward against resistance. This directly targets the infraspinatus and teres minor -- the most commonly weak rotator cuff muscles.

exercises.name: Side-Lying External Rotation
exercises.cue: Lie on your side, elbow bent 90 deg. Hold a light dumbbell and rotate forearm toward ceiling. Pause at top. Critical for rotator cuff health.

exercises.name: Face Pulls
exercises.cue: Cable at face height, pull rope to forehead with elbows flared high. Hits rear delts and external rotators. One of the best shoulder health exercises -- do these every session.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.cue: Hold a band at chest height with straight arms. Pull it apart until arms are wide, squeezing shoulder blades together. Builds rear delt and scapular retractor strength.

exercises.name: Scapular Push-Ups
exercises.cue: In a push-up position, without bending elbows, let your shoulder blades come together (sag), then push them apart (protract). Trains serratus anterior -- key for shoulder blade control.

exercises.name: Wall Slide
exercises.cue: Stand with back against wall, arms in a 'goalpost' position. Slowly slide arms up the wall keeping contact throughout. Trains lower trap and scapular upward rotation.

exercises.name: Prone Y-T-W
exercises.cue: Lie face down. Raise arms into Y shape (overhead), then T (out to sides), then W (elbows bent, squeeze). Light weight or bodyweight only. Comprehensive posterior shoulder and scapular strengthener.

PT_AREAS.label: Lower Back
PT_AREAS.overview: Lower back pain in lifters usually stems from tight hip flexors, weak glutes, poor core stability, or overloading before technique is solid. The fix is rarely rest -- it's targeted strengthening of the posterior chain and core while restoring hip mobility.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid heavy loaded spinal flexion (sit-ups, good mornings) and any movement that reproduces sharp pain. Modify deadlifts to Romanian or trap bar variation.

stretches.name: Knee-to-Chest Stretch
stretches.cue: Lie on your back. Pull both knees to your chest and hug them. Gently rock side to side to massage the lower back. Hold the position and breathe deeply.

stretches.name: Piriformis Figure-4
stretches.cue: Lie on back, cross one ankle over opposite knee. Pull the uncrossed leg toward your chest. Tight piriformis can refer pain to the lower back.

stretches.name: Hip Flexor Lunge Stretch
stretches.cue: Low lunge, back knee on floor. Push hips forward. Tight hip flexors pull the pelvis into anterior tilt, compressing the lumbar spine.

stretches.name: Cat-Cow Mobility
stretches.cue: On all fours, move between arching and rounding your back. Slow and controlled. Restores segmental movement in the lumbar spine and reduces stiffness.

stretches.name: Child's Pose
stretches.cue: Sit back on heels, arms extended forward. Let your lower back relax completely. One of the most effective lower back decompression stretches.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.cue: Lie on back, arms up, knees bent 90 deg. Slowly lower opposite arm and leg toward floor without letting lower back arch. Press lower back into floor throughout. Foundational core stability exercise.

exercises.name: Bird Dog
exercises.cue: On all fours, extend opposite arm and leg. Keep hips level -- don't rotate. Hold 2 seconds. Trains multifidus and core stabilizers that directly support the lumbar spine.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.cue: Lie on back, feet flat. Drive hips up, squeeze glutes hard at top. Weak glutes force the lower back to compensate on every hip extension movement.

exercises.name: McGill Curl-Up
exercises.cue: Lie on back, one knee bent. Place hands under lower back to maintain its natural curve. Raise only head and shoulders -- NOT a sit-up. Spinal flexion with neutral lumbar spine.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.cue: Elbow under shoulder, body in straight line. Don't let hips sag. Targets quadratus lumborum -- a key lateral stabilizer of the spine that's often overlooked.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (light)
exercises.cue: Hinge from hips with soft knees, bar close to legs. This pattern strengthens the posterior chain without the spinal loading of a conventional deadlift. Start very light and focus on the hinge.

PT_AREAS.label: Knees
PT_AREAS.overview: Knee pain in athletes typically comes from quad weakness, poor VMO (inner quad) activation, tight IT band, or patellar tracking issues. Strengthening the quads, hamstrings, and glutes while improving mobility usually resolves most common knee complaints.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid deep knee flexion under heavy load until pain-free. Skip leg extensions if you have patellar tendon pain. Never train through sharp or clicking knee pain.

stretches.name: Standing Quad Stretch
stretches.cue: Stand on one leg, pull heel to glute. Keep knees together and torso upright. Tight quads increase patellar compression.

stretches.name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
stretches.cue: Low lunge position. Push hips forward until you feel the front of the rear hip stretching. Hip flexor tightness changes pelvic position and loads the knee differently.

stretches.name: IT Band Foam Roll
stretches.cue: Lie on side, foam roller under outer thigh. Slowly roll from hip to just above the knee. Pause on tender spots. Tight IT band is a leading cause of lateral knee pain.

stretches.name: Calf Stretch
stretches.cue: Place toes on wall or step, heel on floor. Lean in. Tight calves affect ankle dorsiflexion, which causes the knee to compensate during squatting movements.

stretches.name: Seated Hamstring Stretch
stretches.cue: Legs straight, hinge from hips reaching toward feet. Keep back flat. Hamstring tightness increases tension on the back of the knee.

exercises.name: Terminal Knee Extension
exercises.cue: Anchor a band behind you, loop around knee. Stand with slight knee bend. Straighten the knee against the band resistance. Directly targets VMO -- the teardrop-shaped inner quad that's often weak in people with knee pain.

exercises.name: Step-Up
exercises.cue: Step onto a box or step, drive through the heel of the front foot. Control on the way down. Low-impact quad and glute strengthener that loads the knee in a safe range.

exercises.name: Wall Sit
exercises.cue: Back against wall, thighs parallel to floor. Hold. Isometric quad exercise -- particularly effective for patellar tendon pain as isometrics reduce tendon pain signals.

exercises.name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
exercises.cue: Kneel with feet anchored. Lower your body toward the floor by slowly letting your knees straighten. Use hands to catch yourself. One of the most effective hamstring injury-prevention exercises.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercises.cue: Glute bridge but one leg extended. Hip drives up, squeeze glutes. Glute weakness causes inward knee collapse -- this addresses it directly.

exercises.name: Lateral Band Walk
exercises.cue: Band around ankles, slight squat position. Step sideways maintaining band tension. Strengthens hip abductors which control knee tracking.

PT_AREAS.label: Hips / Hip Flexors
PT_AREAS.overview: Hip flexor tightness is almost universal in anyone who sits regularly. It causes anterior pelvic tilt, lower back pain, and reduced glute activation. Hip mobility work improves squat depth, reduces lower back stress, and directly improves athletic performance.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid forcing range of motion in the hip. Progress gradually. Sharp groin or deep hip pain should be assessed by a professional.

stretches.name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Lunge
stretches.cue: Low lunge, back knee on floor. Push hips forward and hold. Tuck your pelvis slightly (posterior tilt) for a deeper stretch. This is the most important hip flexor stretch.

stretches.name: Pigeon Pose
stretches.cue: From a plank, bring one knee toward the same-side wrist. Sink hips toward floor. Stretches both hip flexors and external rotators (glutes/piriformis) simultaneously.

stretches.name: 90/90 Hip Stretch
stretches.cue: Sit with both hips at 90 deg -- one leg in front, one to the side. Lean gently over the front leg. Targets the hip capsule and external rotators comprehensively.

stretches.name: Supine Figure-4
stretches.cue: Lie on back, cross one ankle over the opposite knee. Pull the lower leg toward your chest. Targets piriformis and deep hip external rotators.

stretches.name: Adductor Squat Stretch
stretches.cue: Wide squat stance, elbows on inner knees. Push knees out gently. Stretches the adductors (inner thigh) which are often neglected but restrict hip mobility significantly.

exercises.name: Hip 90/90 Active Rotations
exercises.cue: In 90/90 position, actively rotate between internal and external rotation of the front hip. This builds active control through the hip range of motion -- not just passive flexibility.

exercises.name: Hip Thrust
exercises.cue: Upper back on bench, bar or band over hips. Drive hips to full extension, squeeze glutes hard. This directly counteracts hip flexor dominance by strengthening the hip extensors.

exercises.name: KB Goblet Squat with Pause
exercises.cue: Hold kettlebell at chest. Squat deep and pause 3 seconds at the bottom. Elbows push knees out. This builds active hip flexion strength and mobility simultaneously.

exercises.name: Cossack Squat
exercises.cue: Wide stance. Shift weight to one side, bending that knee while keeping the other leg straight. Lower as deep as comfortable. Exceptional hip and adductor mobility builder.

exercises.name: Fire Hydrant
exercises.cue: On all fours, raise one knee out to the side like a dog at a fire hydrant. Keep hips level. Targets hip abductors and external rotators -- often weak from prolonged sitting.

exercises.name: Single-Leg RDL
exercises.cue: Balance on one leg, hinge at hip pushing the other leg back. Keep back flat. Trains hip hinge mechanics, glute strength, and balance simultaneously.

PT_AREAS.label: Neck / Upper Traps
PT_AREAS.overview: Neck and upper trap tension is almost always postural -- forward head position, rounded shoulders, and weakness in the deep neck flexors and lower traps. Phone and desk use are the primary culprits. The fix is a combination of releasing the tight muscles and strengthening the opposing weak ones.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid heavy shrugs and any overhead work that causes neck pain. Never crack your own neck forcefully.

stretches.name: Neck Side Tilt
stretches.cue: Tilt head toward one shoulder, ear to shoulder. Let gravity do the work -- don't pull. Hold and breathe. Feel the stretch along the opposite side of the neck.

stretches.name: Neck Rotation Stretch
stretches.cue: Turn head to look over one shoulder as far as comfortable. Hold. Then turn the other way. Don't force the end range.

stretches.name: Levator Scapulae Stretch
stretches.cue: Tilt head forward and to one side at about 45 deg. Gentle pressure with your hand. Targets the levator scapulae -- the muscle that runs from neck to shoulder blade and is chronically tight in desk workers.

stretches.name: Upper Trap Stretch
stretches.cue: Tilt head to one side. Reach the opposite arm down toward the floor to increase the stretch. Feel the upper trap release.

stretches.name: Pec Minor Stretch
stretches.cue: Place hand high on a wall or doorframe. Rotate body away. Tight pec minor causes shoulder protraction which increases neck strain. Fixing this is as important as stretching the neck itself.

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.cue: Sitting tall, draw your chin straight back (making a double chin). Hold 2 seconds. This retrains the deep cervical flexors which are inhibited by forward head posture. Do these throughout the day.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.cue: Hold band at chest height, arms straight. Pull apart until arms are wide. Builds lower trap and rhomboid strength which pulls shoulders back and relieves neck tension.

exercises.name: Prone Cobra
exercises.cue: Lie face down, arms at sides. Squeeze shoulder blades, lift chest and arms slightly off floor. Rotate palms up. Hold 3 seconds. Directly counteracts the rounded posture that causes neck pain.

exercises.name: Wall Angels
exercises.cue: Stand with back flat against wall. Arms in goalpost position against wall. Slide arms up and down keeping everything in contact with the wall. Trains thoracic mobility and lower trap activation.

exercises.name: Face Pulls
exercises.cue: Cable at face height, pull rope to forehead with elbows high. Strengthens rear delts and external rotators which directly improve posture and reduce forward head position.

PT_AREAS.label: Wrists / Forearms
PT_AREAS.overview: Wrist pain in lifters often comes from tight forearm flexors, poor wrist extension mobility, or grip imbalances. This is especially common with heavy pressing or pulling movements. Consistent mobility and antagonist strengthening resolves most cases.
PT_AREAS.avoidNote: Avoid wrist-loaded exercises during flare-ups. Use wrist wraps as a short-term aid -- not a crutch.

stretches.name: Wrist Extension Stretch
stretches.cue: Extend one arm, use opposite hand to pull fingers back toward you. Hold. This stretches the forearm flexors which are chronically tight from gripping.

stretches.name: Wrist Flexion Stretch
stretches.cue: Extend arm, push fingers down with opposite hand. Stretches the forearm extensors -- often tight from typing and computer use.

stretches.name: Prayer Stretch
stretches.cue: Place palms together in prayer position in front of your chest. Slowly lower hands toward waist keeping palms together. Stretches the wrist flexors through full range.

stretches.name: Wrist Circles
stretches.cue: Extend arms forward. Make large slow circles -- 10 each direction. Lubricates the wrist joint and improves range of motion in all planes.

exercises.name: Wrist Roller
exercises.cue: Hold a wrist roller at shoulder height, arms extended. Roll the weight up, then slowly down. One of the best forearm strengtheners for wrist stability.

exercises.name: Reverse Curl
exercises.cue: Hold barbell or dumbbells with an overhand grip. Curl up. This strengthens the forearm extensors which balance the typically overworked flexors.

exercises.name: Plate Pinch
exercises.cue: Pinch two weight plates together between thumb and fingers. Hold. Builds grip strength and wrist stability from a different angle than standard holds.

exercises.name: Rice Bucket
exercises.cue: Dig hands into a bucket of rice, open and close fingers, rotate wrists. An old PT staple -- exceptional for wrist and finger tendon health.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Chest Doorway Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Place forearms on a doorframe, lean forward until you feel a deep chest stretch. Keep shoulders down.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Cross-Body Shoulder
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Pull one arm across your chest with the opposite hand. Keep shoulder blades level.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Tricep Overhead Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Raise one arm, bend at elbow behind your head. Use opposite hand to gently push elbow back.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Neck Side Tilt
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Tilt your head toward one shoulder, let gravity do the work. Don't force it.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Wall Pec Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Place one hand on a wall at shoulder height, rotate your body away until you feel the stretch across your chest.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Lat Hang
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Hang from a bar with both hands. Let your body weight stretch your lats. Slight hollow body position.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Child's Pose
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Sit back on your heels, arms extended forward. Walk hands out as far as comfortable. Breathe deeply.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Doorway Bicep Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Place palm flat on wall behind you at shoulder height, arm straight. Rotate body away slowly.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Cat-Cow
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: On all fours -- arch your back up (cat), then dip it down and lift chest (cow). Move with your breath.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Thread the Needle
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: On all fours, slide one arm under your body along the floor. Rest on your shoulder and breathe.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Hip Flexor Lunge
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Drop into a low lunge, back knee on floor. Push hips forward and hold. Keep torso tall.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Standing Quad Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Stand on one leg, pull heel to glute. Keep knees together. Use a wall if needed for balance.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Seated Hamstring Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Sit on floor with legs straight. Hinge from hips, reach toward feet. Keep back flat -- don't round.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Pigeon Pose
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: From a plank, bring one knee forward toward your wrist. Lower hips toward floor. Breathe and relax.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Calf Wall Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Place toes on a wall, heel on floor. Lean into the wall keeping leg straight. Feel the calf stretch.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Lying Glute Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Lie on your back. Cross one ankle over the opposite knee. Pull the uncrossed leg toward your chest.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Adductor Squat Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Wide stance squat position, elbows on inner knees. Gently push knees out and hold.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Thoracic Rotation
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Sit cross-legged, place one hand behind head. Rotate your elbow up toward the ceiling. Keep hips square.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: Wrist Circles
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: Extend arms forward. Make large slow circles with both wrists -- 5 each direction.

ALL_STRETCHES.name: World's Greatest Stretch
ALL_STRETCHES.cue: From a lunge, place same-side hand on floor, rotate opposite arm to ceiling. Hold, then switch legs.

cues: Retract shoulder blades and squeeze them together
Feet flat on floor, slight arch in lower back
Bar path: touch lower chest, press back at slight angle
Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width
Bench Press.tip: Think 'push the bar away from you' not 'push yourself into the bench'

cues: Set bench to 30-45 deg
Dumbbells at chest level, elbows at ~75 deg angle
Press up and slightly inward at the top
Control the descent -- 2 second lower
Incline Dumbbell Press.tip: Lower incline (30 deg) hits more chest; higher (45 deg) shifts to shoulders

cues: Stand or sit, bar starts at upper chest
Brace core tight before each rep
Press straight up, slightly back at lockout
Don't flare elbows too wide -- keep ~45 deg
Overhead Press.tip: Squeeze glutes to protect lower back on heavy sets

cues: Slight bend in elbows throughout
Raise to shoulder height -- no higher
Lead with your elbows, not your wrists
Slow and controlled, no momentum
Lateral Raises.tip: Tilt dumbbells slightly so pinky is higher than thumb at top

cues: Elbows pinned to your sides -- don't let them flare
Full extension at bottom, squeeze hard
Control the cable on the way back up
Lean slightly forward at the hips
Tricep Pushdowns.tip: If elbows drift back, reduce the weight

cues: Keep upper arms vertical -- elbows pointed at ceiling
Lower dumbbell/bar behind head slowly
Only forearms move -- upper arms are locked
Full stretch at bottom, full extension at top
Overhead Tricep Ext.tip: Best exercise for the long head -- the biggest part of your tricep

cues: Bar over mid-foot, hip-width stance
Hinge at hips first, then bend knees to grip
Chest up, back flat -- 'proud chest'
Drive floor away, keep bar dragging up your shins
Deadlift.tip: Take a big breath into your belly and brace before every single rep

cues: Hinge 45 deg forward, bar hangs below chest
Pull to lower chest/upper belly -- not shoulders
Squeeze shoulder blades together at top
Lower controlled -- don't drop the weight
Barbell Row.tip: Think 'elbow to back pocket' to activate lats better

cues: Start from dead hang -- full arm extension
Depress and retract shoulder blades first
Pull elbows toward your hips, not just down
Chin clears bar -- full rep
Pull-Ups.tip: Crossing your ankles and squeezing keeps your body stable

cues: Set cable at face height or slightly above
Pull rope to your forehead -- hands to ears
Elbows stay flared high throughout
Pause and squeeze at the end of each rep
Face Pulls.tip: One of the most important exercises for shoulder health -- don't skip it

cues: Neutral grip (palms facing each other)
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Curl up, full range of motion
Control the negative -- 2 seconds down
Hammer Curls.tip: Hits the brachialis which pushes the bicep up, making arms look thicker

cues: Set bench to 45-60 deg, sit back fully
Arms hang straight down at start
Curl up slowly -- stretch is the point here
Don't swing or shorten the range
Incline Dumbbell Curl.tip: The stretch at the bottom is what makes this one special -- use lighter weight

cues: Bar on traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar)
Stance slightly wider than hips, toes 15-30 deg out
Break at hips and knees simultaneously
Drive knees out over toes on the way up
Squat.tip: Depth goal: hip crease below parallel. Use depth you can maintain with good form

cues: Slight bend in knees -- keep it consistent
Hinge at hips, push them straight back
Bar stays close to legs the entire way down
Feel a deep hamstring stretch, then drive hips forward
Romanian Deadlift.tip: This is a stretch exercise -- go slow on the way down

cues: Feet shoulder-width, mid-to-high on platform
Lower until knees are at ~90 deg or less
Don't lock out knees at the top -- keep tension
Press through your heels
Leg Press.tip: Higher foot placement = more glutes. Lower = more quads

cues: Step forward, lower back knee toward floor
Front shin stays vertical -- knee over ankle
Push off front heel to step through
Keep torso upright throughout
Walking Lunges.tip: Take long enough strides to get knee close to the floor

cues: Lying: pad just above heels
Curl heels to glutes -- full range
Squeeze hamstrings at top for 1 second
Lower slowly -- 3 second negative
Leg Curl.tip: Plantar-flex your feet (toes pointed) to get a slightly better contraction

cues: Full stretch at bottom -- heel below platform level
Rise all the way up on the ball of your foot
Pause 1 second at the top
Slow negatives -- 3-4 seconds down
Calf Raises.tip: Calves need high volume and full range -- don't bounce at the bottom

cues: Set cables at chest height or above
Slight bend in elbows -- lock it in throughout
Bring hands together in a hugging arc, not a press
Squeeze hard at the centre, slow return
Cable Fly.tip: Focus on the stretch at the end of each rep -- that's where growth happens

cues: Lean slightly forward to hit chest more
Lower until shoulders are below elbows
Elbows flare slightly outward
Drive back up without fully locking out
Dips.tip: Upright torso = more tricep. Forward lean = more chest

cues: Chest flat against pad -- eliminate all momentum
Pull elbows straight back, squeeze at the top
Full stretch at the bottom each rep
Don't shrug your traps -- keep them down
Chest Supported Row.tip: Best row variation if your lower back fatigues easily on other rows

cues: Wide overhand grip, lean back slightly
Pull bar to upper chest -- not behind neck
Drive elbows down and back
Full stretch at top -- let lats lengthen
Lat Pulldown.tip: Think 'elbows to back pockets' to feel lats instead of biceps

cues: Sit tall, slight knee bend, chest up
Pull handle to lower chest/upper belly
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the end
Return with control -- don't let the stack yank you
Seated Cable Row.tip: Pause 1 second at full contraction to maximize muscle activation

cues: Upper arm flat on pad throughout
Full extension at the bottom -- don't cheat the range
Curl to full contraction, pause at top
Slow on the way down -- 3 seconds
Preacher Curl.tip: Eliminates cheating completely -- great for building the bicep peak

cues: Sit on a bench, elbow braced on inner thigh
Curl slowly, supinate wrist at the top
Pause and squeeze at peak contraction
Lower all the way down each rep
Concentration Curl.tip: Keep the rep slow -- speed kills the stimulus here

cues: Lie flat, bar directly above face
Only forearms move -- upper arms stay vertical
Lower bar toward forehead or just behind head
Press back up in a slight arc
Skull Crushers.tip: Slightly angling upper arms back from vertical hits the long head better

cues: Grip shoulder-width -- not too narrow
Elbows stay close to your sides throughout
Lower to lower chest, press straight up
Don't flare elbows on the way down
Close-Grip Bench.tip: Great compound tricep builder -- goes heavy better than isolation work

cues: Start with palms facing you at chin level
Rotate palms outward as you press up
Full overhead lockout at top
Reverse rotation on the way back down
Arnold Press.tip: The rotation recruits more of the front and side delt than a standard press

cues: Hinge forward 45 deg, slight bend in elbows
Raise arms out to the sides -- think T shape
Lead with your elbows, not your hands
Squeeze rear delts at the top
Rear Delt Fly.tip: Most people go too heavy here -- use a weight you can actually feel

cues: Hold dumbbells or barbell at your sides
Shrug straight up -- no rolling
Hold at the top for 1-2 seconds
Lower slowly all the way down
Shrugs.tip: Rolling your shoulders does nothing -- just go straight up and squeeze

cues: Adjust pad to just above ankles
Extend fully and hold 1 second at the top
Lower with control -- don't drop
Toes slightly inward can increase inner quad activation
Leg Extension.tip: Great finisher after squats -- pump the quads with 15-20 reps

cues: Upper back on bench, bar over hip crease
Feet hip-width, toes slightly out
Drive hips straight up -- squeeze glutes hard at top
Chin tucked, don't hyperextend lower back
Hip Thrust.tip: The best exercise for glute development -- don't skip it on leg day

cues: Kneel facing cable, rope at top pulley
Hold rope at temples, don't pull with arms
Crunch down -- think elbow to knee
Hold contraction 1 second, control return
Cable Crunch.tip: Keep hips still -- all the movement should come from your spine flexing

cues: Elbows under shoulders, body in straight line
Squeeze abs, glutes, and quads simultaneously
Don't let hips rise or sag
Breathe steadily -- don't hold your breath
Plank.tip: 30 seconds with perfect tension beats 2 minutes of sagging

cues: Hands slightly wider than shoulders
Body in straight line head to heels
Lower chest to floor -- full range
Push explosively up, control down
Push-Ups.tip: Elevate feet to increase difficulty; hands elevated to reduce it

cues: Form inverted V with hips high
Lower head toward floor between hands
Push back up to start
Closer feet = harder
Pike Push-Ups.tip: The more vertical your torso, the more shoulder activation

cues: Hands form a diamond shape under chest
Elbows track back close to ribs
Full range -- chest to hands
Keep core braced throughout
Diamond Push-Ups.tip: One of the hardest bodyweight tricep exercises -- go slow

cues: Feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out
Arms forward for balance, chest up
Sit back and down -- depth to parallel or below
Drive through heels to stand
Bodyweight Squat.tip: Pause 2 seconds at the bottom for extra difficulty

cues: Squat to parallel, then explode upward
Soft landing -- absorb through hips and knees
Go straight back into the squat
Arms swing for momentum
Jump Squat.tip: Land quietly -- that's the sign of good control

cues: Rear foot elevated on bench
Front foot far enough forward that knee stays behind toe
Lower back knee toward floor
Drive through front heel to stand
Bulgarian Split Squat.tip: One of the best bodyweight leg exercises -- as hard as any barbell work

cues: Lie on back, feet flat, knees bent
Drive hips straight up -- squeeze glutes at top
Hold 1-2 seconds at the top
Lower slowly -- don't let hips touch the floor between reps
Glute Bridge.tip: Single-leg version is a great progression

cues: Set bar low, lie under it, heels on floor
Body in straight line -- don't let hips sag
Pull chest to bar squeezing shoulder blades
Lower with control -- full arm extension
Inverted Row.tip: Raising feet increases difficulty; bending knees makes it easier

cues: Supinated (palms toward you) grip
Start from dead hang
Pull until chin clears bar
Control the negative -- 3 seconds down
Chin-Ups.tip: Supinated grip means more bicep -- great for arm development too

cues: From standing: squat down, kick feet back to plank
Perform a push-up (optional)
Jump feet back to squat, then jump up
Clap overhead at the top
Burpee.tip: Scale by removing the jump or push-up when fatigued

cues: Start in push-up position, core braced
Drive one knee toward chest, then switch
Keep hips level -- don't bounce them up
The faster you go the more cardio; slower = more core
Mountain Climbers.tip: Great as a finisher -- 30 seconds at the end of any session

cues: Lie on back, arms overhead
Press lower back into floor
Lift shoulders and legs off floor slightly
Hold -- breathe steadily, don't let lower back arch
Hollow Body Hold.tip: This is the foundation of calisthenics core work

cues: Hinge at hips -- not a squat
Snap hips forward explosively to drive the bell
Bell floats to chest height -- arm-powered swing is wrong
Hinge back again as the bell descends
KB Swing.tip: The power comes from your hips, not your arms or lower back

cues: Hold KB at chest, elbows pointing down
Squat deep -- elbows track inside knees
Stay tall through the chest
Drive heels through the floor to stand
KB Goblet Squat.tip: Great for learning squat mechanics -- the counterbalance helps depth

cues: Clean KB to rack position (resting on forearm)
Press straight overhead from rack
Lower with control back to rack, then swing down
Keep wrist straight throughout
KB Clean and Press.tip: One of the best full-body kettlebell movements

cues: Hold KB in both hands or one hand
Hinge from hips pushing them back
Keep back flat and KB close to legs
Drive hips forward to stand
KB Romanian Deadlift.tip: Single-leg version is a serious balance and strength challenge

cues: Set bench 15-30 deg decline, feet hooked under pad
Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width
Lower bar to lower chest with control
Press straight up -- don't arch excessively
Decline Barbell Press.tip: Best movement for lower chest thickness -- often skipped but very effective

cues: Lie flat, dumbbells at chest, elbows on floor
Pause when triceps touch floor -- removes leg drive completely
Press up and in slightly at the top
Elbows at about 45-60 deg from torso
Dumbbell Floor Press.tip: The floor stop eliminates momentum -- forces pure pressing strength

cues: Adjust seat so handles are at chest height
Slight bend in elbows, locked throughout
Bring arms together in a hug -- not a press
Squeeze hard at the centre, slow controlled return
Pec Deck / Machine Fly.tip: Focus on the stretched position at the start -- that's where the pec works hardest

cues: Stand tall, dumbbells at thighs
Raise both arms to eye level -- no higher
Keep a very slight elbow bend throughout
Lower slowly -- 2-3 seconds down
Front Raises.tip: Avoid swinging -- if you need momentum the weight is too heavy

cues: Cable at hip height, stand side-on
Cross body and pull cable out and up to shoulder height
Pinky leads at the top -- thumb points down
Pause 1 second at the top
Cable Lateral Raise.tip: Constant cable tension beats dumbbells for lateral raise stimulus

cues: Face the machine pad, chest against it
Grasp handles with arms parallel to floor
Pull elbows back and out -- squeeze rear delts
Don't use momentum -- this is a slow, deliberate movement
Reverse Pec Deck.tip: One of the few machine exercises with no real free-weight equivalent

cues: Hinge forward 45 deg, upper arm parallel to floor
Only the forearm moves -- like a pendulum
Fully extend at the top and pause 1 second
Keep upper arm locked -- don't let it drop
Tricep Kickbacks.tip: Best for the lateral head -- go lighter and feel the contraction

cues: Knee and hand braced on bench, back flat
Pull elbow past torso -- think 'elbow to hip pocket'
Pause at top, feel the shoulder blade retract
Full stretch at the bottom every rep
Single-Arm DB Row.tip: Don't rotate the torso to get the weight up -- that's your lower back working, not your lat

cues: Stand at cable station, slight forward lean
Slight elbow bend, locked throughout
Drive bar to hips using lats -- arms stay rigid
Slow return, feel the lat stretch overhead
Straight-Arm Pulldown.tip: One of the best lat isolation exercises -- really feel the muscle working

cues: Set pins at knee height or just below
Setup identical to deadlift -- bar over mid-foot
Drive hips forward, squeeze traps hard at lockout
This is a partial range -- go heavier than your deadlift
Rack Pull.tip: Great for overloading the lockout and building trap thickness

cues: Barbell in landmine -- stand perpendicular at far end
Hinge forward, grip the end of the bar with one hand
Pull elbow high and out -- flares more than a DB row
Brace your other hand on your knee for support
Meadows Row.tip: The angle creates a unique lat stretch that regular rows can't match

cues: Low pulley, face the cable stack
Elbows pinned at your sides throughout
Curl to full contraction -- supinate at the top
Lower slowly -- constant tension the whole way
Cable Curl.tip: Cables keep tension at the bottom -- dumbbells lose it there. Great combination

cues: Overhand (pronated) grip on barbell or cable
Keep elbows pinned, curl to chest
Full extension at the bottom each rep
Control the negative -- forearms work hard here
Reverse Curl.tip: The brachialis sits under the bicep -- training it pushes the bicep peak higher

cues: Feet shoulder-width on platform, low placement
Keep lower back against pad throughout
Full depth -- knee over toes
Press through heels -- don't let knees cave
Hack Squat.tip: Lower foot placement = more quad. Use it as a quad finisher after regular squats

cues: Kneel with feet anchored under something stable
Lower body slowly -- 4-5 second descent
Use hands to catch yourself at the bottom
Pull back up or use hip extension to return
Nordic Hamstring Curl.tip: One of the most effective hamstring strength exercises -- also the most humbling

cues: Wide stance, toes pointed out 30-45 deg
Grip inside your legs, arms vertical
Push knees out hard as you pull
Bar stays close to body -- hip drive at the top
Sumo Deadlift.tip: Hips are closer to the bar than conventional -- reduces lower back stress

cues: Hold support for balance
Lean body back as knees travel forward over toes
Lower until knees nearly touch floor
Full terminal knee extension at the top
Sissy Squat.tip: Pure quad isolation -- the knee-forward position is intentional, not bad form

cues: Pad rests just above knees, slight knee bend
Full stretch at the bottom -- heel below platform
Rise all the way up, pause 1 second at top
Bent knee targets the soleus more than standing raises
Seated Calf Raise.tip: Most people neglect the soleus -- it's the deeper calf muscle that gives thickness

cues: Full range -- let the pads open wide at the start
Squeeze legs together through full range
Slow and controlled -- don't slam the pads together
Hold 1 second at the top contraction
Adductor Machine.tip: Weak adductors cause knee cave in squats -- worth training directly

cues: Pad behind ankle or knee depending on machine
Drive leg back to full hip extension
Squeeze glute hard at the top -- don't hyperextend lower back
Slow return to start
Glute Kickback Machine.tip: Hip extension is the primary glute function -- isolate it here

cues: Step onto box, drive through heel of the leading foot
Keep torso upright -- don't lean forward excessively
Control the step down -- don't drop
Add dumbbells when bodyweight is easy
Step-Ups.tip: Great single-leg strength builder that's easier on the knees than lunges

cues: Stand centred in the trap bar
More knee bend than conventional deadlift
Load is balanced over your feet -- more natural position
Powerful hip drive, squeeze glutes at lockout
Trap Bar Deadlift.tip: The neutral grip and balanced load make this the most beginner-friendly deadlift variant

cues: Wide hand placement, both arms extended
Shift weight to one side -- that arm bends, other stays straight
Lower chest to the bending side
Alternate sides each rep
Archer Push-Ups.tip: The hardest bodyweight push-up progression before one-arm push-ups

cues: Feet elevated on bench or chair
Body in a straight line from feet to head
Lower chest toward floor -- full range
The incline shifts load to the upper chest and shoulders
Decline Push-Ups.tip: The higher the elevation, the more shoulder-press-like it becomes

cues: Hands wider than shoulder-width
Elbows flare more than standard -- that's intentional
Lower until chest touches floor
Focus on feeling the chest stretch at the bottom
Wide Push-Ups.tip: Width shifts load from triceps to the chest -- great variation for chest development

cues: Hands at hip level, fingers pointing back
Lean body forward significantly before bending elbows
Keep body rigid as a plank
The forward lean is what creates the difficulty
Pseudo Planche Push-Ups.tip: One of the best strength builders for both chest and shoulder stability

cues: Lower to one side of your body
Slide chest across the bottom position to the other side
Push up from the opposite side
Keep hips and core level throughout the slide
Typewriter Push-Ups.tip: The lateral slide creates a unique stretch and contraction -- very effective

cues: Kick up against a wall for support
Arms locked out, press through the shoulders
Try to get ears between arms -- not head forward
Work toward 30-second holds before progressing off wall
Handstand Hold.tip: Builds overhead pressing strength that transfers directly to overhead press and dips

cues: Hands on chair behind you, legs extended
Lower until elbows reach 90 deg
Elbows track straight back -- not flaring outward
Press up without locking out completely
Tricep Dips (Chair).tip: Keeping legs straight makes this significantly harder than knees-bent version

cues: Lower to bottom push-up position
Hold with chest just off the floor -- 3-5 seconds
Maintain full tension through the whole body
Breathe steadily -- don't hold your breath
Isometric Push-Up Hold.tip: Isometrics build tendon strength and stability that traditional reps can't -- great for injury prevention

cues: Start in push-up position
Push explosively -- hands leave the floor
Land softly, absorb with bent elbows
Focus on the power of the push, not the height
Plyometric Push-Ups.tip: Develops explosive pressing power -- great for sport performance

cues: Push-up position, feet hip-width apart
Tap opposite shoulder with one hand while balancing
Keep hips square -- resist the urge to rotate
The wider your feet the easier the balance
Shoulder Taps.tip: The goal is zero hip rotation -- all movement should stay above the waist

cues: One hand forward of shoulder, one hand back
This shifts more load to the forward arm
Lower chest to the floor -- full range
Switch hand positions between sets to balance both sides
Staggered Push-Ups.tip: Exposes left/right strength imbalances that bilateral push-ups hide

cues: Perform a push-up, then at the top rotate to side plank
Raise top arm to ceiling -- full T position
Hold 1 second, return and repeat other side
Keep hips level throughout the rotation
T Push-Ups.tip: Combines pressing strength with rotational stability -- efficient full-upper-body movement

cues: Grip wider than shoulder-width -- overhand
Pull elbows down and back -- not just down
Chin clears the bar, full dead hang at bottom
Wider grip shortens the lat's range but increases width stimulus
Wide-Grip Pull-Ups.tip: Emphasises lat width -- combine with close-grip for complete lat development

cues: Parallel grip -- palms facing each other
Often easiest on the shoulder of all pull-up grips
Full dead hang, pull to chin or chest
The neutral grip often allows a better lat connection than overhand
Neutral-Grip Pull-Ups.tip: Best pull-up variation if shoulder pain prevents overhand or underhand grips

cues: Hang from bar and raise legs to parallel with floor
Hold the L position throughout the pull
Pull chin to bar while keeping legs up
Start with just the L-sit hold before combining
L-Sit Pull-Ups.tip: One movement that trains pulling strength and core simultaneously -- extremely efficient

cues: Dead hang from bar -- fully relaxed
Without bending elbows: depress and retract shoulder blades
You'll rise slightly -- hold 2 seconds
Lower back to full hang -- this is one rep
Scapular Pull-Ups.tip: The foundation of healthy shoulder mechanics -- do these before every pull-up session

cues: Set bar at waist height, hang underneath
Body in straight line from feet to shoulders
Pull chest to bar -- same motion as a row
Lower with control -- full arm extension each rep
Australian Pull-Up.tip: Perfect stepping stone between rows and full pull-ups -- scale by raising bar height

cues: Loop a towel around a fixed pole or door handle
Hold both ends with a neutral grip
Curl toward your shoulders -- full range
The grip challenge is part of the exercise
Towel Curls.tip: Builds forearm and grip strength alongside bicep stimulus -- functional carryover

cues: Stand on the band, shoulder-width
Curl to full contraction -- constant tension throughout
Full extension at the bottom -- don't short the range
Slower reps than with dumbbells for maximum stimulus
Band Bicep Curl.tip: Bands keep tension through the full range unlike free weights -- use them as a supplement

cues: Lie face down, arms extended overhead
Simultaneously lift arms and legs off the floor
Hold the peak position 2-3 seconds per rep
Breathe out on the lift
Superman Hold.tip: One of the few exercises that directly trains the spinal extensors without load -- great prehab

cues: Hinge at the hips over the pad
Lower torso toward the floor -- full stretch
Drive hips forward and up to full extension
Squeeze glutes at the top -- not lower back
Back Extension.tip: Focus on glute contraction at the top rather than hyperextending the spine

cues: Parallel grip on the bar
Pull up to one side -- chin goes over one hand
Lower, then pull up to the opposite side
Alternate sides each rep -- creates rotational demand
Commando Pull-Ups.tip: The alternating direction trains rotational stability that standard pull-ups miss

cues: Pull chin up to one hand
Shift body laterally so chin moves to the other hand
Slow and controlled across the top
This is extremely advanced -- build regular pull-up volume first
Typewriter Pull-Up.tip: Only attempt once you can do 15+ clean pull-ups -- it's a true strength test

cues: Lie on back, one leg extended straight
Drive working heel into floor, push hips up
Squeeze glute hard at the top -- hold 1-2 seconds
Keep hips level -- don't let opposite hip sag
Single-Leg Glute Bridge.tip: Fixes glute imbalances that cause knee pain and lower back issues

cues: Step wide to one side -- stay upright
Sit into the stepping leg -- other leg stays straight
Drive through the heel to push back to start
Keep toes pointed forward on the straight leg
Lateral Lunge.tip: The lateral direction trains hip abductors and adductors that forward lunges miss

cues: Step back onto your toes -- not the heel
Lower the back knee toward the floor
Front shin stays vertical -- knee over ankle
Push off the front heel to return
Reverse Lunge.tip: Easier on the knees than forward lunges -- good starting point if knees are sensitive

cues: Very wide stance, toes pointed out at 45 deg
Shift weight to one side, bending that knee deeply
Other leg stays straight, foot flat or heel raised
Go as deep as your mobility allows
Cossack Squat.tip: One of the best combined strength and mobility exercises -- builds hip range of motion

cues: Back flat against wall, thighs parallel to floor
Knees directly over ankles -- not forward
Hold the position -- breathe steadily
Add weight across thighs to progress
Wall Sit.tip: Isometric quads -- particularly effective for patellar tendon health and rehab

cues: Balance on one leg with a slight knee bend
Hinge from the hip -- free leg floats straight back
Keep hips square -- don't open to the side
Touch the floor or reach toward it with opposite hand
Single-Leg RDL.tip: The single-leg demand reveals hip stability weaknesses that deadlifts hide

cues: Hinge forward 90 deg at the hips -- hands on bench
Rise up on toes through full range
Full stretch at the bottom -- heel below toe level
The hip-hinged position increases the range vs standing raises
Donkey Calf Raise.tip: Greater range of motion than standing calf raises -- more effective at same load

cues: Hold a parallel squat position
Small pulses -- only a few inches of range
Keep tension constant -- don't lock out or drop too deep
The burn is the point -- metabolic fatigue
Squat Pulse.tip: Great finisher after heavy squats -- empties the tank with zero additional joint stress

cues: On all fours, hands under shoulders, knees under hips
Raise one knee out to the side -- like a dog at a fire hydrant
Keep hips level -- don't tilt
Hold 1 second at the top, lower with control
Fire Hydrants.tip: Directly targets the hip abductors which are often weakened by sitting

cues: Step one foot behind and across to the opposite side
Lower the back knee toward the floor
Hips stay square -- just the leg crosses
Drive off the front foot to return
Curtsy Lunge.tip: Hits the glute medius from a unique angle -- important for hip stability

cues: Stand in front of box, feet hip-width
Quarter-squat and swing arms back, then explode
Land softly in a quarter-squat -- absorb through hips and knees
Step down -- don't jump down, risk of Achilles injury
Box Jumps.tip: Power development -- rest fully between sets, quality over quantity

cues: Hop laterally from one foot to the other
Land on the outside foot and reach opposite hand toward it
Hold the single-leg landing 1 second -- balance matters
Increase the distance to increase the challenge
Skater Jumps.tip: Trains the frontal plane -- most strength work is sagittal, this fills the gap

cues: Lie on back, arms straight up, knees bent 90 deg
Lower opposite arm and leg toward floor simultaneously
Press lower back into floor throughout -- don't arch
Exhale as you extend -- breathe deliberately
Dead Bug.tip: The lower back pressing into floor is the whole point -- if it arches, reduce your range

cues: On all fours, hands under shoulders, knees under hips
Extend opposite arm and leg simultaneously
Keep hips completely level -- no rotation
Hold 2 seconds at full extension
Bird Dog.tip: Trains the small stabilizing muscles of the spine that heavy lifts don't reach

cues: Elbow under shoulder, body in a straight line
Stack feet or stagger them for stability
Don't let the hip sag toward the floor
Breathe steadily -- squeeze glutes and obliques
Side Plank.tip: The QL is the main target -- it's a key stabilizer that's often undertrained

cues: Kneel on a pad, wheel under shoulders
Roll forward slowly -- lower back stays flat
Go only as far as you can without your back arching
Pull back with your abs -- not by pushing with your arms
Ab Wheel Rollout.tip: One of the hardest ab exercises -- the long lever arm makes it brutally effective

cues: Dead hang from bar -- no swinging
Raise legs until thighs are parallel (or higher)
Lower with control -- don't just drop
Tuck version is easier; straight leg is harder
Hanging Leg Raise.tip: If you swing, your hip flexors are taking over -- slow down and use a dead hang start

cues: Lie flat, arms and legs extended
Simultaneously lift legs and upper body -- meet in the middle
Keep legs straight -- bend at the hip not the knee
Lower both slowly to complete one rep
V-Ups.tip: A progression from crunches -- if your lower back lifts off when you lower, reduce range

cues: Sit with knees bent, lean back to 45 deg
Rotate the torso from side to side
Feet can be on floor or raised for more difficulty
Touch hands or a plate to the floor on each side
Russian Twists.tip: Add a pause on each side to increase the challenge -- the rotation should come from the torso, not just the arms

cues: Lie on back, hands behind head, knees bent
Bring opposite elbow to opposite knee
Extend the other leg straight and low
Rotate from the torso -- not just the elbow
Bicycle Crunch.tip: Slow the movement down -- speed kills the stimulus and lets momentum take over

cues: Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat
Hands behind head or crossed on chest
Lift shoulder blades off the floor -- not your full back
Lower with control -- don't just drop
Crunch.tip: Think about shortening the distance between your ribcage and hips, not sitting up

cues: Run in place, driving knees to hip height
Pump arms in opposition to legs
Stay on the balls of your feet
Keep your core braced throughout
High Knees.tip: The faster the pace, the higher the heart rate -- great as a warm-up or work interval

cues: Start with feet together, arms at sides
Jump feet out and raise arms overhead simultaneously
Jump back to starting position
Keep a soft bend in the knees on landing
Jumping Jack.tip: Classic warm-up movement -- focus on landing softly to protect your knees

cues: Start in a split stance lunge position
Jump and switch legs in the air
Land in lunge with opposite leg forward
Absorb landing through the heel and midfoot
Jumping Lunge.tip: Keep your torso upright and land as quietly as possible to protect the knees

cues: Step forward, lower back knee toward the floor
Front shin stays vertical -- knee over ankle
Push off the front heel to return
Keep your torso tall and upright throughout
Lunge.tip: Think about dropping down, not stepping forward -- it keeps the knee safer

cues: Lie flat, hands under your lower back for support
Raise legs to 90 deg keeping them straight
Lower slowly -- stop just before they touch the floor
Press lower back into the floor throughout
Leg Raise.tip: The slower the lower, the harder the ab work -- don't rush the descent

cues: Start in high plank position
Tap opposite shoulder with one hand, then switch
Keep hips completely still -- no rotation
Squeeze your core and glutes to stabilize
Plank Tap.tip: The challenge is resisting rotation -- if your hips are swaying, widen your feet

cues: Start in high plank, core braced
Push hips up and back into downward dog
Hold briefly at the top, then return to plank
Keep arms straight throughout
Plank to Downdog.tip: The transition is the point -- it challenges shoulder stability and core control simultaneously

cues: High plank position, hands under shoulders
Tap one shoulder with the opposite hand
Return and repeat on the other side
Keep hips completely square to the floor
Shoulder Tap.tip: Wider foot stance makes it easier -- narrow it as you get stronger

cues: Feet wider than shoulder width, toes turned out 45 deg
Sit straight down -- keep chest up
Knees track over toes throughout
Drive up through the heels
Sumo Squat.tip: The wide stance shifts more emphasis to the inner thighs and glutes vs a regular squat

cues: Start standing, slight bend in knees
Jump and pull knees toward chest
Land softly on the balls of your feet
Immediately absorb and repeat
Tuck Jump.tip: Land as quietly as possible -- loud landings mean you're not absorbing the impact properly

cues: Stand facing the box, feet hip-width
Bend knees, swing arms back, then jump
Land with both feet on top, knees soft
Step down -- don't jump down to protect the knees
Box Jump.tip: Focus on sticking the landing -- power is useless without control

cues: Start with feet hip-width, slight knee bend
Swing arms back then drive forward
Land with both feet simultaneously, knees soft
Measure from takeoff line to heel landing
Broad Jump.tip: Think about driving your hips forward, not just jumping up -- horizontal power is the goal

cues: Athletic stance, knees slightly bent
Push off the outside foot to shuffle sideways
Stay low throughout -- don't bounce up
Touch the ground at each end to mark the distance
Lateral Shuffle.tip: The lower you stay, the more glute and quad activation -- resist the urge to stand up

cues: Drive knees up as high and fast as possible
Pump arms aggressively in opposition
Stay on the balls of your feet -- heels don't touch
Maintain a slight forward lean
Sprint in Place.tip: Maximum effort is the whole point -- short bursts at 100% are more effective than long moderate ones

cues: Keep elbows close to your sides
Rotate the rope with your wrists -- not your shoulders
Jump just high enough to clear the rope
Land softly on the balls of your feet
Jump Rope.tip: Consistent rhythm beats high jumps -- focus on timing over height

cues: Bar starts over mid-foot, hip-width stance
First pull: push floor away, bar stays close to legs
Second pull: extend hips explosively, shrug and pull
Catch in quarter squat, elbows forward
Power Clean.tip: The power comes from the hip extension -- the arms just guide the bar up

cues: Hold dumbbells at shoulder height
Squat down keeping chest up and knees out
Drive up explosively and press overhead in one motion
Lock out arms at the top before descending
Dumbbell Thruster.tip: The squat drives the press -- use leg power to initiate the press, don't do them as two separate movements

cues: High plank with dumbbells as handles
Row one dumbbell to your hip, keeping hips level
Return and repeat on the other side
Brace hard to prevent rotation
Renegade Row.tip: Start with lighter weight than you think -- stabilizing with one hand is much harder than it looks

cues: Hinge at hips, dumbbells hanging down
Drive elbows back powerfully and explosively
Allow dumbbells to travel slightly higher than normal
Control the return -- only the pull is explosive
Explosive Row.tip: Think about trying to touch your elbows behind your back -- creates full retraction

cues: Start with dumbbells at sides, palms forward
Curl to shoulders, then rotate and press overhead
Lower from overhead back to shoulders, then curl down
Control every phase -- 4 movements in one
DB Curl to Press.tip: The rotation at the top engages more shoulder muscle than a neutral press

cues: Start with KB between legs, hinge position
Drive hips forward explosively
Guide the bell overhead in a smooth arc -- not a swing
Lock arm at the top with wrist straight
KB Snatch.tip: The bell should float up, not be muscled up -- hip power does the work

cues: Lie flat, KB pressed overhead, eye on the bell
Rise to elbow, then to hand, then to kneeling
Stand up fully, then reverse every step to lie back down
Keep the KB locked overhead throughout every transition
KB Turkish Get-Up.tip: Go slow -- this is a technique exercise, not a conditioning one. Speed comes after mastery

cues: Retract shoulder blades and squeeze together
Feet flat on floor, slight arch in lower back
Bar path: touch lower chest, press back at slight angle
Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width
Barbell Bench Press.tip: Think 'push the bar away from you' not 'push yourself into the bench'

cues: Bar on upper traps, feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out
Brace core hard before descent, push knees out over toes
Break at hips and knees simultaneously, hit parallel or below
Drive through the whole foot, lead with chest on the way up
Barbell Squat.tip: Think 'spread the floor apart with your feet' to keep knees tracking correctly

cues: Set bench 30-45 degrees
Bar touches upper chest, elbows at 75 degree angle
Press straight up, slight backward lean
Control descent 2-3 seconds
Incline Barbell Press.tip: Lower incline hits more chest; higher incline shifts more to shoulders

cues: Supinated grip shoulder-width, elbows pinned to sides
Curl through full range, squeeze hard at top
Lower under control, full extension at bottom
No swinging — initiate with bicep, not momentum
Barbell Curl.tip: Lean slightly forward at the bottom to increase stretch on the bicep

cues: Semi-supinated grip reduces wrist strain
Elbows stay pinned at sides throughout
Curl to chin height, pause and squeeze at top
3-second descent for maximum tension
EZ Bar Curl.tip: EZ bar reduces supination slightly but is easier on the wrists — use it when straight bar hurts

cues: Barbell at front rack position, feet hip-width
Slight knee bend (dip), drive legs to initiate
Use leg drive to start bar moving, press arms to lockout
Receive bar under control, reset stance
Push Press.tip: The dip and drive comes from the legs — think of throwing the bar up, not pressing it

cues: Hinge at hips, chest against pad if available, neutral spine
Drive elbows back past torso, squeeze shoulder blades hard at top
Full stretch at bottom, chest stays braced
Don't round lower back to add range
T-Bar Row.tip: Neutral grip handles hit mid-back harder; wider grip shifts more to lats

cues: Dumbbells at chest height, elbows at 75 degree angle
Press up and slightly together at top, don't fully lock out
Lower under control, get full stretch at bottom
Neutral grip (palms facing each other) reduces shoulder stress
DB Bench Press.tip: Dumbbells allow greater range of motion than barbell — use that extra stretch

cues: Dumbbells at ear height, elbows at 90 degrees
Press straight up, bring dumbbells slightly together at top
Don't flare elbows too wide — keep at 75 degrees
Control descent, full range each rep
DB Shoulder Press.tip: Arnold press variation adds rotation for more complete shoulder development

cues: Alternating or simultaneous, supinate wrist as you curl
Full extension at bottom, full squeeze at top
Keep upper arm vertical, don't swing shoulder
Slow down the eccentric for more growth stimulus
DB Curl.tip: Supinate (twist wrist outward) at the top of each rep to fully contract the bicep

cues: Slight bend at elbow, lean forward 10-15 degrees
Lead with elbows, not wrists — think 'pour water from a jug'
Lift to shoulder height, pause 1 second
Control the descent — the negative builds side delts
DB Lateral Raise.tip: Leaning slightly forward shifts the load to the true lateral head, not front delt

cues: Bench at 30-45 degrees, dumbbells at chest level
Press up and slightly inward at the top
Full stretch at the bottom — let shoulder blades spread
Control 2-3 second descent
Incline DB Press.tip: Lower incline (30 deg) hits more chest; higher (45 deg) shifts to shoulders

cues: Bench at 45-60 degrees, arms hanging straight down
Curl from full stretch, do not swing shoulder forward
This is one of the best bicep exercises — the stretch is the key
Full extension between reps
Incline DB Curl.tip: The incline position stretches the long head of the bicep uniquely — go lighter than standing

cues: Both hands gripping one dumbbell overhead, elbows close to ears
Lower behind head, feel tricep stretch
Extend to full lockout at top, squeeze triceps
Keep core braced to protect lower back
Overhead DB Ext.tip: The overhead position puts the long head of the tricep in full stretch — the most important part

cues: Hold dumbbell or kettlebell at chest height, elbows pointing down
Feet shoulder-width, toes out 30 degrees
Squat deep between knees, keep chest tall
Drive through heels, elbows push knees out at bottom
Goblet Squat.tip: Great for quad depth — the counterweight lets you sit lower than a bodyweight squat

cues: Cable at low pulley, rope attachment, neutral grip
Curl both hands up simultaneously, split rope at top
Keep elbows pinned to sides throughout
Full extension at bottom
Rope Hammer Curl.tip: Splitting the rope at the top adds a squeeze that isolates the brachialis hard

cues: Lie on bench, one dumbbell at chest, other arm braced or free
Core braces hard to prevent rotation — that's the challenge
Press straight up, control descent with full stretch
Complete all reps one side before switching
Single-Arm DB Press.tip: The anti-rotation demand is what makes this different from regular DB press — brace hard

cues: Dumbbell at shoulder, feet hip-width, slight knee bend
Press straight up, core resists lateral lean
The free side wants to tip — brace against it
Complete all reps one side, then switch
Single-Arm OHP.tip: Your core works overtime stabilising against the offset load — this is the point

cues: Cable at chest height, step away for tension, slight forward lean
One arm flies across body, feel chest stretch at start
Squeeze pec hard at end point, slow return
Brace core to resist cable pulling you sideways
Single-Arm Cable Fly.tip: Unilateral flies allow you to feel each pec individually — notice left/right differences

cues: Stand side-on to cable or hold dumbbell, slight lean away
Lead with elbow, raise to shoulder height
Pause at top, slow 3-second descent
Cable version provides constant tension unlike dumbbells
Single-Arm Lateral Raise.tip: Leaning slightly away from the cable creates even better lateral delt isolation

cues: Cable at high pulley, single handle, elbow pinned to side
Push handle straight down to full extension
Squeeze tricep hard at lockout, slow return
No elbow flare — keep it tight to the body
Single-Arm Tricep Pushdown.tip: Single arm forces each tricep to work independently — great for fixing strength asymmetries

cues: Cable at mid-height, sit or stand, slight forward lean
Drive elbow back past torso, rotate slightly to increase range
Squeeze shoulder blade at end, full stretch at start
The rotation adds lat engagement beyond standard cable row
Single-Arm Cable Row.tip: Allow some trunk rotation on the pull — this increases range of motion and lat activation

cues: Single handle on pulldown cable, sit square to machine
Pull elbow down and back, lean back slightly
Full stretch at top, squeeze lat at bottom
Alternate or complete all reps one side first
Single-Arm Lat Pulldown.tip: Single-arm allows more trunk lean and lat stretch than bilateral — maximise the range

cues: Cable at face height, single handle, step back for tension
Pull to face with elbow high and out to the side
Squeeze rear delt and external rotators at end
Slow return, maintain tension throughout
Single-Arm Face Pull.tip: High elbow position is crucial — dropping the elbow shifts load away from rear delts

cues: Cable at low pulley, D-handle, face the machine
Curl from full extension, squeeze bicep at top
Slow 3-second descent for maximum stimulus
Cable provides constant tension unlike dumbbells
Single-Arm Cable Curl.tip: Cable curl keeps tension on the bicep at full extension — unlike dumbbells which go slack

cues: Place one hand on bench behind you, other arm on thigh or free
Lower body by bending that arm, push back to straight
Keep torso close to bench to target tricep not shoulder
Full range — elbow to 90 degrees minimum
Single-Arm Tricep Dip.tip: Closer hand placement = more tricep; hands wider = more chest involvement

cues: One foot centred on platform, other leg off or held
Lower to 90-degree knee bend, push through heel
Keep lower back pressed into pad throughout
Single leg exposes and corrects side-to-side strength differences
Single-Leg Press.tip: Go significantly lighter than your bilateral leg press — imbalances will surprise you

cues: Lie prone on curl machine, one leg anchored, one curling
Curl through full range, squeeze at peak contraction
Lower slowly 3 seconds — the eccentric builds hamstring size
Keep hips pressed into pad
Single-Leg Curl.tip: Single leg curls reveal hamstring imbalances — both legs should feel similarly strong

cues: Upper back on bench, one foot on floor, other leg raised
Drive through the grounded heel to full hip extension
Pause 1 second at top, squeeze glute hard
Don't let hips drop or rotate
Single-Leg Hip Thrust.tip: The non-working leg creates an anti-rotation demand — brace your core throughout

cues: Stand on one foot on edge of step, heel below level of step
Rise to full extension, pause at top
Slow 3-4 second descent for maximum stretch
Hold for balance if needed — the balance challenge is secondary to calf work
Single-Leg Calf Raise.tip: The stretch at the bottom is where calf growth happens — don't skip the range

cues: Adjust seat so handles align with lower chest
Grip handles, retract shoulder blades before pressing
Press to near full extension, don't fully lock out
Slow controlled return, feel chest stretch
Chest Press Machine.tip: Machine allows heavier loading with less stability demand — great for high-rep volume sets

cues: Adjust seat so handles are at ear height
Press straight up without shrugging shoulders
Control descent, full range each rep
Neutral or pronated grip depending on machine
Machine Shoulder Press.tip: Machine press removes the stability demand — useful for high-rep volume without fatigue spillover

cues: Cable at high pulley, rope or bar, elbows pinned to sides
Push down to full extension, squeeze triceps hard
Slow 2-3 second return, keep upper arm stationary
Lean slightly forward for better leverage
Tricep Pushdown.tip: The key is keeping upper arms locked — any movement forward takes load off triceps

cues: Face away from cable, rope at high pulley, elbows by ears
Extend arms overhead to lockout, squeeze triceps
Lower behind head, feel stretch, don't let elbows flare
Core braced — don't arch lower back
Overhead Cable Ext.tip: Overhead position maximally stretches the long head — the largest part of the tricep

cues: Cables at low setting, step forward with slight lean
Arc hands upward and together in front of face
Squeeze chest hard at the top, slow return
Keep slight bend in elbow throughout
Cable Fly (Low to High).tip: Low-to-high angle targets the upper chest the same as an incline press

cues: Facing cable, attachment at high pulley, arms extended overhead
Pull bar down in arc toward thighs, keep arms almost straight
Squeeze lats at bottom, slow controlled return to stretch
Feel the lat stretch at the top — don't rush it
Cable Pullover.tip: One of the few exercises that isolates the lat without bicep assistance

cues: Balls of feet on edge, full range of motion
Lower heel below step level for maximum stretch
Rise to full extension, squeeze hard, pause 1 second
Gastrocnemius is best hit with straight knee
Standing Calf Raise.tip: Full range is everything — half reps on calves produce minimal growth

cues: Step forward into lunge, back knee approaches floor
Front shin stays vertical, knee tracks over toes
Drive through front heel to stand, step forward with other leg
Keep torso upright throughout
Walking Lunge.tip: The continuous movement demands more balance and hip stability than static lunges

cues: Bar on upper traps, feet hip-width, soft knee bend
Hinge at hips, push them back as you lower chest
Lower until hamstrings are tight (not past), maintain neutral spine
Drive hips forward to return, squeeze glutes at top
Good Morning.tip: Think 'push hips back' not 'lower chest down' — it's a hip hinge not a bow

cues: Upper back on bench or floor, feet flat, knees bent
Drive through heels to full hip extension
Squeeze glutes hard at top, pause 1 second
Add weight (plate on hips) when bodyweight feels easy
Hip Thrust (BW).tip: Tilting feet slightly outward increases glute activation at the top

cues: Attach weight via belt or hold dumbbell between feet
Dead hang start, pull until chin clears bar
Squeeze lats at top, full extension at bottom
Slow 3-second descent
Weighted Pull-Ups.tip: Adding even 10-20lb changes the stimulus significantly — use a belt for best experience

cues: Belt with weight, or dumbbell between crossed ankles
Lower until upper arms parallel to floor or slightly below
Lean slightly forward for chest focus, upright for tricep focus
Drive up to near lockout, controlled descent
Weighted Dips.tip: Slight forward lean shifts emphasis to lower chest; staying upright keeps it on triceps

cues: Partner places plate on upper back, or use weighted vest
Standard push-up form: straight body, elbows at 75 degrees
Full range — chest to floor, full extension at top
If form breaks, reduce weight immediately
Weighted Push-Ups.tip: A 45lb plate on your back is roughly equivalent to a 135lb bench press for most people

cues: Hands on bench behind, legs extended, body straight
Lower by bending elbows to 90 degrees
Keep torso close to bench — don't drift forward
Press back up, avoid shrugging shoulders
Tricep Dips.tip: Feet on floor is easier; feet elevated or weight on lap increases difficulty

cues: Loop towel around pull-up bar, hold ends
Hang with arms extended, curl body up by bending elbows
Full range — arms straight at bottom, tucked at top
Harder than it looks — grip and bicep work together
Towel Curl.tip: The unstable grip makes every rep a grip and bicep challenge — great for functional strength

cues: Band anchored at face height, step back for tension
Pull band to face with elbows high and out
External rotate at the end — thumbs back
Slow controlled return
Face Pull (Band).tip: High elbows and external rotation at the end are both essential — don't skip either

cues: Bar at waist height, hang beneath it, body straight
Pull chest to bar, squeeze shoulder blades
Lower slowly with full control
Elevate feet to increase difficulty
Australian Pull-Ups.tip: Easier than pull-ups, harder than inverted rows — perfect bridge exercise

cues: Wide grip on bar, pull up toward one hand while extending the other
Alternate which side you pull toward each rep
Keep core tight — don't swing or kip
Slower is harder — own the range
Archer Pull-Ups.tip: One step below a one-arm pull-up — the extended arm still assists but far less

cues: Generate hip drive by swinging body in hollow/arch position
Use momentum to assist chin over bar
Control the descent — don't just drop
Used for volume, not max strength development
Kipping Pull-Ups.tip: Kipping is a skill for volume work — pair with strict pull-ups for best development

cues: Dead hang start, pull as explosively as possible
Goal: hands leave bar at the top
Catch bar on the way down, immediately re-hang
Full lockout between reps
Explosive Pull-Ups.tip: The explosive intent trains fast-twitch fibres differently from controlled pull-ups

cues: Standard push-up position, explosive push off floor
Clap hands before landing, land with soft elbows
Focus on explosive intent, not clap speed
Tuck elbows on landing to absorb safely
Clap Push-Ups.tip: The benefit is in the explosive concentric — even if you don't clear the ground much

cues: Pike position, small jump from feet to drive
Press at top of jump for assist
Good stepping stone to handstand push-up
Feet land softly, reset for next rep
Jump Pike Push-Ups.tip: Think of it as a plyometric OHP — the goal is shoulder power, not just reps

cues: Lunge position, jump up and switch legs in air
Land softly in opposite lunge, absorb through bent knee
Keep torso upright, arms assist the jump
Land heel-to-toe to protect knees
Jumping Lunges.tip: Soft landing is more important than jump height — protect your knees

cues: Lunge position, jump up and switch legs in air
Land softly in opposite lunge, absorb through bent knee
Keep torso upright, arms assist the jump
Land heel-to-toe to protect knees
Jump Lunge.tip: Soft landing is more important than jump height — protect your knees

cues: Lie on back, feet flat, standard glute bridge setup
Drive hips up explosively — attempt to leave the ground
Land softly, immediately go into next rep
Squeeze glutes hard even on explosive reps
Explosive Glute Bridge.tip: The explosive version trains fast-twitch glute fibres — different stimulus from slow holds

cues: Stand on both feet, jump using only calf/ankle plantar flexion
Minimal knee bend — isolate the ankle drive
Land on balls of feet, immediately spring again
Fast rhythm, high reps
Calf Jump.tip: Restricting knee bend forces the calves to do all the work — it's harder than it looks

cues: Stand on band, palms supinated, curl to chin height
Squeeze bicep at top, slow 3-second descent
Keep elbows pinned to sides
Band tension increases through range — hardest at top
Band Curl.tip: Band resistance peaks at the top unlike dumbbells — great complementary stimulus

cues: Band anchored at face height, pull to face with elbows high
External rotate at end — thumbs behind ears
Slow return maintaining tension
Great for rotator cuff health
Band Face Pull.tip: Do these often — most people's internal rotators are overdeveloped vs external

cues: Stand on band, neutral grip, curl to shoulder height
Keep wrists neutral throughout — don't supinate
Slow descent, full extension at bottom
Targets brachialis and forearms more than standard curl
Band Hammer Curl.tip: Neutral grip hits the brachialis and brachioradialis more than supinated grip

cues: Band anchored low, sit on floor, loop around feet
Drive elbows back past torso, squeeze shoulder blades
Full stretch at front, hold 1 second at back
Keep torso stable — minimal swinging
Band Seated Row.tip: Sit tall and pull elbows to pockets — rowing to the belly button hits lats harder

cues: Band anchored overhead, face the anchor point
Pull band down to chin level, drive elbows to hips
Squeeze lats at bottom, slow return to full stretch
Lean back slightly for better lat activation
Lat Pulldown (Band).tip: Imagine pushing your elbows into your back pockets — that mental cue lights up the lats

cues: Stand on band, neutral grip at sides
Raise to shoulder height, lead with elbows
Band tension builds through range — resist at the top
Slow 3-second descent
Band Lateral Raise.tip: Bands peak resistance at the top where the delt is strongest — good muscle-curve match

cues: Hold TRX, ring, or pole for balance support
One leg extended forward, squat down on standing leg
Keep heel flat, use minimal arm support
Build toward unassisted over time
Pistol Squat Assist.tip: The goal is to reduce assistance each session until you're fully unassisted

cues: In pike position (hips high, legs straight), one arm at a time
Hold position, feel shoulder and core work
Builds toward single-arm handstand prep
Keep free arm across body or behind back
Single-Arm Pike Hold.tip: The balance challenge here is significant — start with 5-10 second holds and build

cues: One hand on elevated surface (book, block), one on floor
Standard push-up form, more load on the lower hand side
Switch sides between sets
Bridge between push-ups and archer push-ups
Uneven Push-Ups.tip: The height difference shifts more load to one side — great unilateral progression

cues: Hands on two surfaces behind, feet elevated in front
Lower deeper than standard dip for more chest
Control descent, press back to straight arms
Very deep range — respect shoulder mobility limits
Bulgarian Dips.tip: The deeper range hits chest significantly more than regular bench dips

cues: Standard plank/push-up position
After each rep, tap opposite shoulder with one hand
Keep hips square — resist rotation
The anti-rotation demand is the key training stimulus
Shoulder Tap Push-Ups.tip: Wider foot stance makes balance easier — narrow it as you improve

cues: Start in downward dog position, swoop forward and up through cobra
Reverse the motion back to downward dog
Continuous flowing movement, no stopping
Shoulder mobility and chest both benefit
Hindu Push-Ups.tip: Traditional in Indian wrestling training — great for shoulder health alongside pressing

cues: Hands on elevated surface (bench, step)
Body straight, lower chest to surface
Reduces load vs floor push-ups — good for volume or beginners
Can increase difficulty by lowering the surface height
Incline Push-Ups.tip: The lower the surface, the harder it gets — floor is hardest in this family

cues: Bar at waist height, hang beneath it, one arm gripping
Free arm across chest or behind back
Pull chest to bar with single arm
Feet on floor is easier; elevate for harder
Single-Arm Inverted Row.tip: Significantly harder than two-arm — grip and lat strength are exposed

cues: Hang from bar with one hand, thumb over bar for security
Hold position for time — build from 5 seconds
Both a strength exercise and tendon conditioning
Don't jump into this — build grip strength first
Single-Arm Chin Hang.tip: This is serious tendon work — progress slowly, don't rush time under tension

cues: Band anchored low, single arm pull to hip
Drive elbow back past torso, squeeze shoulder blade
Allow slight trunk rotation for full range
Complete all reps one side, then switch
Single-Arm Band Row.tip: Even with bands, focus on the lat squeeze — don't just pull with the bicep

cues: Stand on band with one foot, single arm curl
Squeeze bicep at top, slow 3-second descent
Keep elbow pinned, no swinging
Band tension increases through range
Single-Arm Band Curl.tip: Single arm allows you to focus on each bicep independently — notice strength differences

cues: Balls of feet on edge, heels below step level
Rise to full extension, pause 1 second at top
Slow 3-4 second descent for maximum stretch
Single or double leg depending on difficulty needed
Calf Raise.tip: Full range and slow descent are the two non-negotiables for calf development

cues: Band anchored overhead, face the anchor, pull down to chin
Drive elbows to hips, squeeze lats at the bottom
Slow return to full overhead stretch
Lean back slightly for better lat activation
Band Lat Pulldown.tip: Imagine pushing your elbows into your back pockets — that cue lights up the lats

cues: On hands and knees or standing with cable, drive leg back
Squeeze glute hard at full extension, pause 1 second
Control the return — don't let gravity do the work
Keep hips square, don't rotate
Glute Kickback.tip: Think 'push the heel to the ceiling' not 'swing the leg back' for better glute isolation

Bench Press.equipment_note: No barbell? Use dumbbells for same movement — greater ROM at bottom.

Incline Dumbbell Press.equipment_note: No dumbbells? Use barbell on incline or resistance bands.

Overhead Press.equipment_note: No barbell? Dumbbell OHP is equally effective — more shoulder stability work.

Lateral Raises.equipment_note: No dumbbells? Cable lateral raise or resistance band lateral raise.

Overhead Tricep Ext.equipment_note: No cable? Use dumbbell or EZ bar overhead extension.

Tricep Pushdowns.equipment_note: No cable? Use resistance band anchored above head.

Close-Grip Bench.equipment_note: No barbell? Close-grip dumbbell press or diamond push-ups.

Cable Fly.equipment_note: No cable? Use dumbbells on flat bench or resistance bands.

Arnold Press.equipment_note: No dumbbells? Use barbell press or machine shoulder press.

Dips.equipment_note: No parallel bars? Use two sturdy chairs or a bench behind you.

Skull Crushers.equipment_note: No EZ bar? Use dumbbells or do overhead tricep extension instead.

Decline Barbell Press.equipment_note: No decline bench? Flat barbell with slight arch, or dumbbell on flat.

Deadlift.equipment_note: No barbell? Trap bar deadlift or heavy dumbbell RDL.

Barbell Row.equipment_note: No barbell? Use two dumbbells or a cable machine.

Pull-Ups.equipment_note: No pull-up bar? Use a sturdy door frame bar or lat pulldown machine.

Lat Pulldown.equipment_note: No cable machine? Use resistance bands anchored overhead.

Seated Cable Row.equipment_note: No cable? Use resistance band anchored in front or DB row.

Face Pulls.equipment_note: No cable? Use resistance band anchored at head height.

T-Bar Row.equipment_note: No T-bar? Use landmine setup with barbell in corner, or Meadows row.

Pendlay Row.equipment_note: No barbell? Dumbbell dead-stop row from floor, or cable row from seated.

Chest Supported Row.equipment_note: No incline bench? Lie on flat bench with dumbbells, or use cable.

Single-Arm DB Row.equipment_note: No dumbbells? Use cable machine with single handle attachment.

Rack Pull.equipment_note: No rack? Set plates on plates to raise bar height, or do trap bar DL.

KB Swing.equipment_note: No kettlebell? Use a dumbbell held vertically by one end.

Romanian Deadlift.equipment_note: No barbell? Use dumbbells — actually more comfortable for most.

Hammer Curls.equipment_note: No dumbbells? Use rope attachment on cable, or EZ bar with neutral grip.

Incline Dumbbell Curl.equipment_note: No incline bench? Do standing curl with slight lean back for stretch.

Squat.equipment_note: No barbell? Goblet squat with dumbbell/KB, or belt squat if available.

Leg Press.equipment_note: No leg press machine? Use heavy goblet squat or hack squat.

Hip Thrust.equipment_note: No barbell? Use dumbbell on hips or banded hip thrust.

Leg Curl.equipment_note: No machine? Nordic hamstring curl or slider leg curl on floor.

Leg Extension.equipment_note: No machine? Terminal knee extensions with band, or sissy squat.

Hack Squat.equipment_note: No hack squat machine? Use Smith machine or barbell heel-elevated squat.

Nordic Hamstring Curl.equipment_note: No partner/anchor? Use lat pulldown machine kneeling, or GHD machine.

Trap Bar Deadlift.equipment_note: No trap bar? Regular deadlift with sumo stance, or goblet squat.
